proposals.title: Differential Effects of Sedentary Behavior on Cognitive Performance, Brain Structure, Functional Connectivity, and Dementia Risk in Older Adults: A Multi-Modal Investigation
proposals.motivation: Public health guidelines for older adults often treat sedentary behavior (SB) as a uniform risk factor for poor brain health. However, pre-2026 research, including dose-response analyses of physical activity, suggests health outcomes may depend on the specific 'density' and pattern of behaviors. It remains unknown whether prolonged SB equally impairs all domains of brain health or has selective, disproportionate effects on specific neural systems and cognitive functions, which is critical for developing targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher levels of objectively measured sedentary behavior will have domain-specific, non-uniform associations with brain health, showing stronger negative relationships with executive function and hippocampal volume compared to processing speed or overall cortical thickness, and that these neural markers will differentially mediate the association between SB and long-term dementia risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study with 300 older adults (65-80 yrs). SB will be measured via 7-day accelerometry (ActiGraph GT9X), quantifying total volume, patterns, and breaks. At baseline and 24-month follow-up, we will assess: 1) Cognition (NIH Toolbox, episodic memory, executive function tasks), 2) Brain structure (3T MRI: cortical thickness, hippocampal volume), and 3) Resting-state functional connectivity (default mode and frontoparietal networks). Dementia risk will be tracked via medical records. Analyses will use multivariate linear mixed-effects models and structural equation modeling to test for differential effect sizes across domains and mediation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond the assumption of uniform risk by providing the first integrated, multi-modal evidence on the specificity of SB's neural and cognitive correlates. It will inform more precise public health guidelines by identifying which brain health domains are most vulnerable to sedentariness. The findings will also elucidate potential mechanistic pathways linking SB to clinical dementia outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links SB to general cognitive decline, no pre-2026 study has simultaneously and comparably tested its differential associations across distinct brain health domains (cognition, structure, function, clinical risk) within the same cohort using objective measures, allowing for direct comparison of effect magnitudes and interrelationships.

proposals.title: The Temporal Architecture of Sedentary Behavior: Associations of Bout Duration, Timing, and Breaks with Cognitive Function and Loneliness in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on sedentary behavior (SB) and brain health has predominantly focused on total volume, yet emerging evidence suggests the pattern of accumulation may be critical. This is particularly relevant for older adults, where prolonged, uninterrupted sitting may exacerbate cognitive decline and social isolation, independent of total sedentary time. Understanding the specific temporal features of SB—such as bout length, daily timing, and break frequency—is necessary to move beyond the FITT principle and develop more nuanced public health guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longer mean sedentary bout duration and later peak sedentary timing (evening) will be independently associated with poorer executive function and greater loneliness in older adults, even after controlling for total sedentary time and physical activity. Conversely, we hypothesize that a higher frequency of sedentary breaks will be associated with better cognitive outcomes and lower loneliness.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+ years). Participants will wear a thigh-worn activPAL4 accelerometer for 7 days to objectively measure SB patterns (total duration, bout length, breaks). Cognitive function will be assessed via a computerized battery (NIH Toolbox, flanker, list sorting). Loneliness will be measured using the UCLA Loneliness Scale. We will employ multivariate linear regression and structural equation modeling to test the independent associations of SB pattern metrics (bout duration, timing, breaks) with cognitive and loneliness outcomes, controlling for covariates including age, education, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated analysis of how the temporal microstructure of daily sedentary behavior relates to both cognitive and psychosocial health in aging. It will generate empirical evidence to refine public health guidelines beyond simple 'move more, sit less' messaging, potentially identifying specific behavioral targets (e.g., breaking up sitting every 30 minutes, avoiding evening sedentariness) for interventions aimed at preserving brain health and reducing loneliness.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant paradigm by treating total sedentary time as a covariate rather than the primary exposure, focusing instead on its patterning. It uniquely bridges Kramer's work on physical activity dose-response and loneliness by examining how the *when* and *how* of sitting may serve as a novel behavioral pathway linking sedentary lifestyles to cognitive and social well-being.

proposals.title: Personalizing Physical Activity for Cognitive Health: A Data-Driven Approach to Match Multimodal Phenotypes with Optimal Intervention Types in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity guidelines for older adults are one-size-fits-all, despite known heterogeneity in cognitive and physical profiles. Pre-2026 research by Kramer and others highlights the need to move beyond generic FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) principles to understand dose-response relationships, but a framework for empirically matching individuals to the specific activity type (e.g., aerobic, resistance, mind-body) that maximizes their cognitive benefit is lacking. This gap limits the efficacy and scalability of exercise as a public health intervention for aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, data-driven phenotypic clusters, derived from baseline cognitive, neural, psychosocial, and physical measures, will differentially predict the magnitude of cognitive improvement from specific, well-defined physical activity intervention types.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month randomized controlled trial with 300 older adults (65+), assigning them to one of three evidence-based intervention arms: aerobic training, resistance training, or tai chi (a mind-body exercise). Deep phenotyping at baseline will include structural/functional MRI, a cognitive battery assessing executive function and memory, actigraphy, and assessments of loneliness, trait mindfulness, and self-control. We will use an unsupervised machine learning approach (Gaussian Mixture Modeling) to identify phenotypic clusters. Subsequently, we will apply supervised learning (Random Forest regression) to model the interaction between cluster membership, intervention type, and post-intervention cognitive change, identifying optimal phenotype-intervention matches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, data-driven algorithm for personalizing physical activity prescriptions for cognitive health in aging. It will advance the field from generic guidelines toward precision exercise medicine. The resulting framework will offer clinicians and public health officials a concrete methodology to maximize the cognitive benefits of physical activity programs for diverse older adults.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines general dose-response relationships, this study uniquely integrates multimodal phenotyping with a comparative intervention design to *predictively match* individuals to optimal activity types, a novel application of machine learning to the personalization of behavioral interventions for cognitive aging.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing the 24-Hour Day: A Compositional Data Analysis of Sleep, Sedentary Time, and Physical Activity on Cognitive Health and Loneliness in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on physical activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep in older adults often treats these behaviors in isolation, ignoring their codependent nature within the finite 24-hour day. This siloed approach limits our understanding of the true dose-response relationships for cognitive health and psychosocial outcomes like loneliness. A compositional framework is essential to model the relative effects of reallocating time between these behaviors, moving beyond simple duration-based guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative composition of a 24-hour day (sleep, sedentary time, light physical activity, moderate-to-vigorous physical activity) is a stronger predictor of cognitive performance and loneliness in older adults than the absolute duration of any single behavior. Specifically, we predict that reallocating time from sedentary behavior to moderate-to-vigorous physical activity will be associated with the greatest cognitive benefit and reduction in loneliness.
proposals.method: We will apply Compositional Data Analysis (CoDA) methods, specifically using isometric log-ratio (ilr) transformations, to 7-day accelerometer and sleep diary data from a cohort of older adults (n>500). Cognitive outcomes (executive function, memory) and loneliness (UCLA Loneliness Scale) will be modeled as functions of the ilr coordinates using multiple linear regression. We will conduct isotemporal substitution models to estimate the effect of theoretically reallocating fixed durations (e.g., 30 minutes) between behavioral components while holding the remaining composition constant.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, mathematically robust framework for analyzing 24-hour activity data, directly informing more holistic public health guidelines for older adults. It will yield specific, evidence-based recommendations for time-use reallocation to optimize both brain health and social-emotional well-being, addressing key themes in Dr. Kramer's work on cognitive aging and loneliness.
proposals.novelty: While CoDA is established in geology and nutrition, its application to the 24-hour activity cycle in cognitive aging research is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts the analytic paradigm from examining behaviors in isolation to modeling their inherent interdependence, offering a more precise method to address the density of behaviors within a finite day.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Associations of Physical Activity, Cathepsin B (CTSB), and Cognitive Decline in Older Adults: A 5-Year Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates that physical activity (PA) benefits brain health in aging, but the underlying molecular mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Cathepsin B (CTSB), a myokine elevated by aerobic exercise, has been linked to neurogenesis and improved memory in animal models, but its long-term role in human cognitive aging is unknown. This study addresses a critical gap by investigating CTSB as a potential mechanistic biomarker linking PA to cognitive trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher levels of habitual physical activity will be associated with sustained elevations in plasma CTSB over time, and that these CTSB levels will mediate the positive relationship between PA and the preservation of cognitive function, particularly in executive function and episodic memory, across a 5-year period in older adults.
proposals.method: We propose a 5-year longitudinal observational study with 300 cognitively healthy older adults (aged 65-80). Annual assessments will include: 1) objective PA measurement via wrist-worn accelerometers (e.g., ActiGraph) analyzed using validated cut-points for intensity; 2) fasting blood draws for plasma CTSB analysis via ELISA; and 3) a comprehensive neuropsychological battery targeting executive function and episodic memory. Data will be analyzed using latent growth curve modeling and multilevel mediation analysis to test within- and between-person associations and mediation effects over time, controlling for covariates like age, APOE status, and baseline fitness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal evidence in humans for CTSB as a mechanistic link between PA and cognitive health, moving beyond simple dose-response (FITT) models. It will yield practical insights for refining exercise guidelines for brain health by identifying a biomarker that could help personalize interventions and monitor their efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kramer and others has established PA-cognition relationships and highlighted the need to understand density and mechanisms, this proposal uniquely integrates a specific molecular candidate (CTSB) into a long-term human aging study, directly testing its mediating role over time—a novel approach not yet undertaken in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Moderating the Mind-Body Connection: A Multi-Cohort Investigation of Fitness, APOE-ε4, and Cardiovascular Health as Moderators of the Physical Activity-CTSB-Cognition Pathway in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that physical activity (PA) benefits cognition, partly through biomarkers like Cathepsin B (CTSB). However, the substantial individual variability in cognitive response to PA remains poorly understood. Identifying key moderators is critical for moving beyond generic PA guidelines towards personalized interventions for cognitive aging, a major public health priority.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of the mediated pathway from PA to cognition via CTSB will be moderated by baseline cardiorespiratory fitness, APOE-ε4 carrier status, and cardiovascular health, such that the relationship will be strongest in individuals with higher fitness, non-carrier status, and better cardiovascular health.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., from the LIFE Study, BLSA) from adults aged 65+. Using a moderated mediation structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will model PA (accelerometry) as the predictor, serum CTSB as the mediator, and cognitive composite scores (executive function, memory) as outcomes. Pre-specified moderators (fitness [VO2 peak], APOE genotype, Framingham risk score) will be tested on the PA→CTSB and CTSB→cognition paths using multi-group and latent interaction analyses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated test of key biological and health moderators of a specific PA-biomarker-cognition pathway. It will generate evidence to refine existing PA guidelines for brain health by identifying subgroups most and least likely to benefit, informing targeted clinical and public health strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines PA-cognition moderators or the PA-CTSB link separately, this proposal uniquely integrates them to test moderation of a specific *mechanistic pathway*, moving beyond simple dose-response to explain heterogeneity in cognitive outcomes.

proposals.title: Plasma Cathepsin B as a Biomarker for Physical Activity-Induced Cognitive Improvement in Older Adults: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Physical activity (PA) guidelines for older adults are based on broad health outcomes, but individual cognitive responses vary widely, creating a need for objective biomarkers to personalize interventions. Pre-2026 research identifies Cathepsin B (CTSB), a myokine released during exercise, as a potential mediator of neurogenesis and memory in animal models, but its translational utility as a peripheral biomarker in humans is unproven. Validating CTSB would provide a crucial tool for quantifying the neurocognitive 'dose' of PA, moving beyond the FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) framework to a more precise, biologically-grounded understanding of dose-response relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increases in plasma CTSB levels following a structured PA intervention will be positively correlated with, and predictive of, improvements in specific cognitive domains (particularly executive function and episodic memory) in sedentary older adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, two-arm randomized controlled trial with 120 sedentary adults aged 65-80. The intervention arm will follow a supervised, moderate-intensity aerobic exercise protocol (e.g., brisk walking), while the active control will engage in stretching and toning. We will collect fasting plasma samples at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months to quantify CTSB via ELISA. A comprehensive cognitive battery will assess executive function, processing speed, and memory. We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze within- and between-group changes, and mediation analyses to test if CTSB changes statistically explain the PA-cognition relationship. Correlations and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis will evaluate CTSB's predictive validity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence on the validity of a blood-based biomarker for monitoring the cognitive efficacy of exercise prescriptions. It will contribute to the development of more precise, biologically-informed PA guidelines for brain health in aging. Furthermore, it will offer a practical tool for researchers and clinicians to objectively assess intervention adherence and efficacy in trials targeting cognitive decline.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links exercise to cognitive benefits and identifies CTSB in animal studies, this proposal directly tests its role as a predictive, peripheral biomarker in a human RCT, bridging molecular mechanisms with real-world cognitive outcomes. It innovatively applies biomarker validation methods from other fields (e.g., oncology) to the problem of quantifying PA 'dose' for brain health.

proposals.title: Dose-Response Relationships of Physical Activity on Dopaminergic Modulation and Executive Function in Older Adults: A Multi-Modal Study
proposals.motivation: While physical activity (PA) is known to benefit brain health and cognition in older adults, the precise dose (type, intensity, duration) that optimally targets the dopaminergic system—a key neurochemical pathway for executive function and reward processing—remains undefined. Current PA guidelines for cognitive health are based on broad FITT principles, lacking neurobiological specificity for dopaminergic pathways, which are critically involved in age-related cognitive decline and motivation for sustained activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a non-linear dose-response relationship exists, where moderate-intensity aerobic exercise of a specific duration (e.g., 30-45 minutes) will induce optimal acute and chronic modulation of dopaminergic markers (e.g., via PET imaging of D2/D3 receptors) and produce the greatest gains in executive function, compared to low- or high-intensity doses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial with 120 older adults assigned to one of four 6-month interventions: low-intensity walking, moderate-intensity aerobic exercise, high-intensity interval training, or a stretching control. Dopaminergic function will be assessed pre- and post-intervention using [11C]raclopride PET to measure D2/D3 receptor binding potential in striatal and prefrontal regions. Cognitive assessments will focus on executive function (task-switching, working memory). We will employ advanced dose-response modeling (e.g., spline regression, response surface analysis) to identify optimal PA parameters, controlling for baseline fitness, age, and genetic factors (e.g., COMT Val158Met).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence directly linking specific PA doses to dopaminergic system modulation and cognitive outcomes in aging, moving beyond gross FITT metrics. It will yield a neurobiologically informed PA prescription to maximize cognitive benefits and could inform interventions targeting apathy and anhedonia in older adults.
proposals.novelty: This proposal integrates direct in vivo neuroimaging of the dopamine system with rigorous dose-response modeling of PA parameters, a novel approach to move from generic 'exercise is good' to a precision framework for cognitive health based on underlying neurochemistry.

proposals.title: Internalizing and Externalizing Problems as Predictors of Non-Adherence to 24-Hour Movement Behavior Guidelines in Older Adults: A Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis
proposals.motivation: Research on 24-hour movement behavior (24-HMB) guidelines for older adults has predominantly focused on how physical activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep influence mental health. However, the reverse causal pathway—where pre-existing mental health problems undermine an individual's ability to adhere to these integrated guidelines—remains largely untested. This gap is critical because understanding this direction is essential for designing effective, personalized interventions for at-risk older adults, particularly given the known bidirectional relationships between behaviors like TV viewing and loneliness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher baseline levels of internalizing (e.g., depression, anxiety) and externalizing (e.g., impulsivity) problems will predict poorer subsequent adherence to 24-HMB guidelines (integrating physical activity, sedentary time, and sleep) over a 12-month period, and that this predictive relationship will be stronger than the reverse pathway of guideline non-adherence predicting later mental health problems.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of 300 older adults (aged 60+). At baseline and 12-month follow-up, we will assess internalizing/externalizing problems using the Adult Self-Report and measure 24-HMB (accelerometry-derived moderate-to-vigorous physical activity, sedentary time, and sleep duration). Adherence to integrated 24-HMB guidelines will be coded as a binary (met/not met) and continuous score. The primary analysis will employ a cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) with structural equation modeling to test the bidirectional temporal relationships, controlling for age, gender, loneliness, and baseline physical health. Sensitivity analyses will use random-intercept CLPM to account for trait-like stability.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence testing the causal direction between mental health and integrated 24-HMB guideline adherence in older adults. It will advance the field beyond simple FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) principles by identifying specific psychological barriers to holistic behavior change. The findings will inform the development of targeted intervention strategies that address mental health to improve movement behavior compliance.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the standard causal model in exercise psychology by positioning mental health as a predictor of guideline adherence, not merely an outcome. It innovatively applies the 24-HMB framework—a pre-2026 advancement in dose-response understanding—to test a novel, clinically significant directional hypothesis regarding behavioral regulation.

proposals.title: Optimizing Cognitive and Affective Benefits in Older Adults: The Dose-Response Relationship of Exercise Break Timing and Duration During Prolonged Sitting
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a recognized health risk for older adults, linked to cognitive decline and increased loneliness. While exercise breaks are recommended, public health guidelines lack specificity on the optimal timing and minimal effective duration of these breaks to counteract the negative effects of sedentary behavior. This gap limits the development of precise, evidence-based interventions for cognitive and brain health in aging populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there is a non-linear dose-response relationship, where short, strategically timed exercise breaks (e.g., 5-minute light walking) introduced earlier in a sitting bout will yield significantly greater improvements in executive function and reductions in state loneliness compared to later or longer-duration breaks of equivalent total activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, within-subjects study with 60 older adults (65+). Participants will complete five 4-hour sitting conditions: control (no break), and breaks of 2, 5, or 10 minutes of light walking initiated at 30, 90, or 150 minutes into the session. Primary outcomes will be pre- and post-session assessments of executive function (task-switching, working memory) and state loneliness. We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze the interaction effects of break timing and duration on cognitive and affective outcomes, controlling for baseline performance and physical activity levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data defining the minimal effective dose and optimal scheduling of exercise breaks to protect cognitive and affective health during sedentary periods. The findings will directly inform the refinement of physical activity guidelines for older adults, moving beyond generic FITT principles to offer actionable, time-based prescriptions for interrupting sedentary behavior.
proposals.novelty: This work integrates Kramer's focus on cognitive aging and dose-response with the understudied temporal dimension of exercise breaks, explicitly testing the interaction between *when* and *how long* a break should be, which is not addressed by existing literature on break frequency or intensity alone.

proposals.title: Differential Cognitive and Affective Benefits of Physical Activity Breaks in Sedentary Older Adults: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Resistance Exercise, Yoga, Standing, and Cycling
proposals.motivation: Sedentary behavior is a major public health concern for older adults, linked to cognitive decline and increased loneliness. While physical activity guidelines emphasize moderate-to-vigorous activity, the acute cognitive and affective impacts of different, feasible 'break' modalities remain unclear. This limits the development of precise, personalized recommendations for interrupting prolonged sitting to support brain health and well-being in aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that different types of physical activity breaks (resistance exercise, yoga, standing, cycling) will have differential, domain-specific effects on post-break executive function, sustained attention, and state affect (including loneliness-relevant mood) in sedentary older adults, with mind-body (yoga) and resistance activities offering unique benefits beyond energy expenditure-matched cycling.
proposals.method: We will conduct a within-subjects, counterbalanced laboratory study with 60 sedentary adults aged 65-80. Participants will complete four experimental sessions, each involving a 2-hour sedentary period interrupted by a 10-minute break of assigned type (light-intensity resistance exercise, hatha yoga, quiet standing, or seated cycling). Pre- and post-break assessments will include the Flanker task (executive control), Psychomotor Vigilance Task (sustained attention), and the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS). We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze the within-subject effects of break type on cognitive and affective outcomes, controlling for baseline performance, cardiovascular fitness, and trait mindfulness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a fine-grained, comparative analysis of break modalities, moving beyond a simple 'activity vs. rest' paradigm to inform more nuanced public health and workplace guidelines. It will directly address the 'density' of health benefits by linking specific break characteristics (e.g., cognitive engagement in yoga, muscle activation in resistance) to specific cognitive and affective outcomes, advancing a precision medicine approach for healthy aging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares sedentary behavior to exercise, this study directly contrasts multiple, equally brief, and practically implementable break types to isolate their unique mechanistic contributions, integrating cognitive, affective, and potential loneliness-related outcomes within Kramer's research framework on activity and aging.

proposals.title: Frontal Network Connectivity as a Mediator of the Relationship Between Cerebral Microvascular Health and Executive Function in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Aging is associated with declines in cerebral microvascular health and executive function, but the specific neural pathways linking them remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Kramer and others has established that physical activity benefits brain health, yet the mechanistic role of specific functional brain networks in translating vascular benefits to cognitive improvements is unclear. Identifying these networks is critical for developing targeted interventions to preserve cognition in older adults.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between cerebral microvascular integrity (measured via arterial spin labeling and white matter hyperintensity volume) and executive function performance is mediated by the functional connectivity of specific frontal networks, particularly the frontoparietal control network and the cingulo-opercular network.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 older adults (65-80 yrs) for a cross-sectional study involving multi-modal MRI (resting-state fMRI, ASL, FLAIR) and a comprehensive cognitive battery focusing on executive functions (task-switching, inhibition, updating). Functional connectivity within and between frontal networks will be quantified using seed-based correlation analysis and independent component analysis (ICA). Mediation analyses will test whether network connectivity metrics (e.g., within-network strength, internetwork coupling) significantly mediate the association between microvascular markers (cerebral blood flow, WMH burden) and executive composite scores, controlling for age, physical activity, and other confounds.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond simple dose-response relationships by identifying the specific frontal network pathways that translate vascular health into cognitive performance. It will provide a mechanistic neural account for the cognitive benefits of vascular health, directly informing the development of more precise physical activity guidelines and cognitive training protocols aimed at network resilience in aging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links physical activity to both brain vascular health and cognition, this proposal uniquely applies formal mediation models to test specific frontal networks as the mechanistic bridge, integrating vascular imaging and functional connectivity in a single pre-2026 analytical framework.

proposals.title: Pupillometric Indexing of Locus Coeruleus-Norepinephrine Activity During Acute Exercise: Linking Noradrenergic Arousal to Prefrontal-Dependent Attention in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Physical activity guidelines for older adults are based on broad health outcomes, but the precise neurocognitive mechanisms linking exercise to improved executive function remain unclear. Pre-2026 research implicates the locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) system in modulating prefrontal cortex (PFC) function and attention, yet non-invasive, real-time measures of LC-NE activity during exercise are lacking. This gap limits our ability to understand the acute, dose-response relationships between exercise intensity and the neurocognitive arousal that may underpin long-term brain health benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute, moderate-intensity aerobic exercise will elicit a quantifiable increase in task-evoked pupil dilation (a validated pupillometric index of LC-NE activity), and that the magnitude of this pupillary response will be positively correlated with post-exercise improvements in performance on a PFC-dependent attentional control task (e.g., the Attention Network Test).
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 healthy older adults (65-80 yrs) for a within-subjects, counterbalanced study with exercise (30-min moderate-intensity cycling) and seated rest control conditions. Pupillometry (using a high-speed, head-mounted eye-tracker) will be recorded during a baseline auditory oddball task (pre- and post-condition) to index phasic LC-NE reactivity. Pre- and post-condition cognitive assessment will include the Attention Network Test (ANT) to measure executive attention. Data analysis will involve extracting pupil dilation time-locked to oddball stimuli, comparing mean dilation amplitude between conditions using repeated-measures ANOVA, and conducting regression analyses to test the relationship between the exercise-induced change in pupillary response and change in ANT executive control scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, mechanistic link between acute exercise and a key neurochemical system (LC-NE) implicated in cognitive enhancement. It will advance the field beyond gross FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) parameters by offering a psychophysiological biomarker to refine dose-response models of physical activity for brain health. The findings could inform more targeted exercise guidelines aimed at optimizing cognitive benefits for an aging population.
proposals.novelty: While pupillometry is established in cognitive neuroscience, its application as a direct measure of exercise-induced LC-NE arousal in older adults is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational fitness-cognition studies to test a specific neurobiological pathway in real time, integrating Dr. Kramer's expertise in exercise, cognition, and aging with cutting-edge, pre-2026 physiological methods.

proposals.title: Cognitive and Affective Impacts of Ultra-Short Physical Activity Breaks in Older Adults: A Feasibility and Dose-Response Study
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity guidelines for older adults, while evidence-based, often present feasibility challenges, potentially contributing to sedentary behavior and associated risks like loneliness and cognitive decline. The efficacy of micro-breaks (1-5 minutes), which are highly integrable into daily life, remains untested against cognitive and affective outcomes, representing a critical gap in translating public health recommendations into practical, scalable interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that brief, frequent bouts of light-to-moderate physical activity (1-5 minutes) will yield immediate, measurable improvements in executive function and state affect, and that the density of these breaks will show a positive dose-response relationship with these benefits in older adults.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 older adults (65+ years) for a 4-week randomized controlled trial with ecological momentary assessment (EMA). Participants will be assigned to one of four conditions: control (no breaks), 1-minute, 3-minute, or 5-minute activity breaks, performed 3 times daily. Using a smartphone app, participants will receive prompts to complete their assigned activity (e.g., stepping in place, bodyweight squats) and will immediately complete brief cognitive tasks (e.g., 2-back, flanker) and affect/loneliness scales via EMA. Pre- and post-intervention assessments will include standard cognitive batteries and trait mindfulness/self-control questionnaires. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to test within- and between-subject effects of break duration and density.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data on the cognitive and affective efficacy of ultra-short activity breaks, directly informing more feasible public health guidelines. It will extend the 'density' framework for physical activity to the micro-temporal scale and explore psychological mediators (e.g., mindfulness, cognitive errors) of break adherence and benefit.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond testing traditional exercise 'sessions' to investigate the minimal effective dose of activity, integrating real-world feasibility with rigorous measurement of cognitive and psychological outcomes in an older adult population.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Semester-Long, Classroom-Based Exercise Break Protocol on Executive Function, Academic Performance, and Classroom Behavior in Emerging Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kramer's work, demonstrates that physical activity benefits cognitive function and brain health across the lifespan, including in older adults. However, the translation of these findings into structured, scalable interventions within real-world educational settings for emerging adults is limited. This study addresses the need for evidence-based guidelines on the implementation and cognitive-academic outcomes of integrating physical activity into the daily structure of higher education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a semester-long protocol of brief, structured exercise breaks integrated into university lectures will lead to greater improvements in students' executive function, academic performance, and on-task classroom behavior compared to a control condition of passive breaks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial across 20 university lecture sections (N=400 students). The intervention group will perform 5-minute, moderate-intensity exercise breaks (e.g., calisthenics, dance) at the mid-point of each 75-minute lecture, three times per week for a 15-week semester. The control group will have a 5-minute passive seated break. Primary outcomes include pre-post changes in a computerized Flanker task (assessing inhibitory control) and Operation Span task (assessing working memory). Secondary outcomes are final course grades and blinded observer ratings of classroom behavior (e.g., time on-task, disruptions). Data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to account for the nested design.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, ecologically valid test of a scalable physical activity intervention in an educational context. It will generate practical evidence for the 'dose-density' of activity needed for cognitive benefits in young adults, directly informing institutional guidelines. The findings will bridge the gap between laboratory studies on exercise and cognition and applied educational practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines exercise in older adults or general fitness in students, this protocol specifically tests the efficacy of embedding frequent, brief bouts of physical activity directly into the academic schedule over a sustained period, moving beyond the FITT principle to address temporal density in a real-world setting.

proposals.title: Passive Mimetics of Exercise: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Heating and Compression on Cerebral Hemodynamics and Cognitive Function in Sedentary Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Physical inactivity is a major risk factor for cognitive decline and vascular dysfunction in older adults, yet many cannot adhere to exercise guidelines due to mobility limitations or motivational barriers. Pre-2026 research by Kramer and others highlights the critical dose-response relationship between physical activity and brain health, but the field lacks accessible interventions for the most sedentary. This study addresses the urgent need to develop passive, low-effort strategies that can simulate key vascular benefits of exercise to support cognitive aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated, acute sessions of passive whole-body heating and intermittent lower-body compression will independently improve cerebrovascular function (e.g., cerebral blood flow velocity, pulsatility index) and enhance performance on executive function tasks in sedentary older adults, mimicking the acute neurovascular effects of moderate-intensity exercise.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-arm, single-blind randomized controlled trial with 90 sedentary adults aged 60-75. Participants will be assigned to: 1) Passive heating (40°C water-perfused suit, 60 min), 2) Intermittent lower-body compression (sequential pneumatic cuffs, 45 min), or 3) a sham/control condition. Each participant will undergo 12 sessions over 4 weeks. Primary outcomes, measured pre- and post-intervention, include middle cerebral artery blood flow velocity and pulsatility index via transcranial Doppler ultrasound, and performance on a computerized task-switching paradigm. Secondary measures include arterial stiffness (pulse wave velocity), endothelial function (flow-mediated dilation), and self-reported loneliness and mindfulness scales. Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models to test for time-by-group interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic comparison of two distinct passive interventions on cerebrovascular and cognitive outcomes in an at-risk, sedentary older population. It will extend the FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) framework by defining a novel 'type' of intervention—passive mimetics—for brain health guidelines. Findings could inform practical, scalable recommendations for adults who cannot engage in traditional physical activity.
proposals.novelty: While heat therapy and compression have been studied separately for peripheral vascular health, their combined investigation as direct neurovascular 'exercise mimetics' for cognitive benefit is novel. This proposal uniquely bridges Kramer's work on physical activity dose-response and psychosocial factors (loneliness, mindfulness) by testing if purely physiological interventions can yield cognitive benefits independent of behavioral engagement.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dose-Response of Post-Task Intervals: A Neurophysiological Investigation of Rest and Low-Intensity Activity for Cognitive and Neural Recovery in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity guidelines for brain health focus on the FITT principle (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) of the activity itself, but largely ignore the critical role of post-activity intervals. Pre-2026 research by Kramer and others highlights the importance of understanding the density and timing of activity for cognitive benefits, yet the specific neural recovery processes (e.g., memory consolidation, glymphatic clearance) optimized by different post-task states—complete rest, passive motor activity, or light cognitive engagement—remain unknown. This gap prevents the development of precise, evidence-based recommendations for structuring daily activity to maximize cognitive resilience in aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct post-task interval states (complete rest, passive motor activity, light cognitive activity) will differentially optimize specific neural recovery processes, as measured by neurophysiological markers, and that these differential effects will be systematically mapped to improvements in subsequent task performance and long-term brain health indicators in older adults.
proposals.method: Using a within-subjects design, older adult participants will undergo controlled cognitive (e.g., demanding working memory) and physical (e.g., moderate-intensity cycling) bouts. Each bout will be followed by one of three counterbalanced post-task intervals: complete rest (eyes-open, seated), passive motor activity (slow treadmill walking), or light cognitive activity (simple visuospatial puzzles). High-density EEG will be used to track event-related potentials (e.g., P300) and oscillatory power (theta, alpha bands) during recovery. A subset will undergo resting-state fMRI scans pre- and post-interval to assess default mode network (DMN) and salience network reconfiguration. Salivary biomarkers (e.g., cortisol, BDNF) will be assayed. Performance on a subsequent, novel cognitive task will serve as the primary behavioral outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the generic 'rest is good' principle by creating an empirical matrix linking specific interval activities to the optimization of distinct neural recovery mechanisms. It will directly extend Kramer's work on activity density and brain health by providing a mechanistic account of the inter-bout recovery period. The findings will inform the next generation of cognitive and physical activity guidelines for older adults, offering prescriptive advice on how to structure rest periods to enhance cognitive benefits and support long-term brain health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines exercise *or* cognitive training effects, this proposal uniquely targets the *interface* between them—the recovery interval—as a modifiable factor for brain health. It applies a rigorous dose-response framework, typically used for exercise intensity, to the qualitative 'dose' of rest, testing the non-obvious premise that not all 'rest' is equivalent and that passive motor or light cognitive activity may be superior for certain recovery processes.

proposals.title: Active Intervals: The Cognitive and Affective Impact of Low-Intensity Activity During Rest Periods in High-Intensity Interval Training for Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kramer's work, establishes the dose-response relationship between physical activity and brain health, yet the critical component of rest intervals within exercise protocols remains underexplored. Current guidelines for older adults often treat these intervals as passive recovery, potentially overlooking an opportunity to enhance cognitive and affective benefits. This gap is significant given the need to optimize time-efficient exercise regimens for cognitive aging and to combat sedentary behaviors linked to loneliness and cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating structured, low-intensity physical activity (e.g., very light walking or balance movements) during the rest intervals of a high-intensity interval training (HIIT) protocol will lead to greater acute and long-term improvements in executive function, episodic memory, and reductions in state loneliness compared to traditional passive rest intervals in healthy older adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-week randomized controlled trial with three arms: HIIT with passive rest (P-REST), HIIT with active rest (A-REST: light activity), and a stretching control. Approximately 120 older adults (65-80 yrs) will undergo pre- and post-assessments of cognitive function (NIH Toolbox, task-switching), affect, and perceived loneliness. The primary analysis will use mixed-design ANOVAs to compare group x time interactions on cognitive outcomes. Secondary analyses will employ mediation models to test if changes in affect or exercise-related cognitive errors during sessions mediate the relationship between interval type and cognitive gains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence directly testing the 'active rest' hypothesis for brain health, moving beyond the FITT principle to refine the density and qualitative nature of exercise bouts. It will yield practical, evidence-based recommendations for structuring interval training to maximize cognitive and psychological benefits in aging, potentially informing future physical activity guidelines for older adults.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares exercise to rest, this proposal innovatively challenges the assumption that intra-exercise rest periods must be passive, investigating the active component of rest density as a novel modulator of the exercise-cognition relationship in older adults.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways and Non-Linear Dose-Response Relationships: A Natural Experiment on the Impact of Urban Green Space Density on Cognitive Function and Loneliness in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including our own, establishes correlational links between physical activity (PA), brain health, and loneliness in older adults, but the causal role of the environmental context—specifically the density of accessible protected areas (PAs) like parks—remains unclear. Understanding the shape of the dose-response relationship (e.g., threshold effects) and the psychological mediators is critical for designing effective public health guidelines and urban planning interventions aimed at promoting cognitive vitality and reducing social isolation in aging populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increases in local PA (park) density will have a non-linear, causal impact on improving cognitive control and reducing loneliness in older adults, and that these effects will be mediated by increased moderate-to-vigorous physical activity and reductions in perceived neighborhood social isolation.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, quasi-experimental design capitalizing on planned urban park developments as natural experiments. A panel of older adults (n=300) living within defined radii of the sites will undergo assessments at baseline and 12 months post-intervention. High-frequency data collection will include GPS/accelerometry to measure PA density exposure and intensity, ecological momentary assessment of loneliness, and lab-based cognitive tasks (executive function, processing speed). Causal inference will be tested using difference-in-differences models with propensity score matching to control for confounders. Non-linear dose-response and mediation will be analyzed using generalized additive models and path analysis within a structural equation modeling framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for the impact of PA density on cognitive and psychosocial outcomes in older adults, moving beyond the established FITT principles. It will directly inform public health guidelines by identifying potential threshold levels of green space access needed for cognitive benefit and clarify the mechanistic roles of behavior and social perception.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its application of causal inference methods to a natural experiment in environmental gerontology, explicitly testing for non-linear effects of PA density rather than simple proximity, and integrating objective biometrics with psychological mediators to unpack the pathway from environment to cognition.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Dose-Response Relationships of Physical Activity Density on Cognitive Function and Loneliness in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity (PA) guidelines for older adults, including the FITT principle (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type), often assume linear dose-response relationships. However, recent conceptual work (e.g., Kramer's 2025 paper) highlights the importance of PA 'density'—the amount of activity per unit time—as a critical but understudied dimension. It is unknown whether the effects of increasing density on cognitive and psychosocial outcomes are linear, or if there are critical thresholds or diminishing returns, which is essential for designing efficient, personalized interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between PA density and improvements in executive function and reductions in loneliness is non-linear, characterized by an initial steep benefit followed by a plateau or threshold effect, and that this pattern differs between cognitive and psychosocial outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month randomized controlled trial with 180 sedentary older adults (65+). Participants will be assigned to one of three aerobic exercise conditions varying in density (e.g., 150 min/week delivered in 3, 5, or 7 sessions) while total volume is held constant. Primary outcomes include pre/post assessments of executive function (task-switching, working memory) and self-reported loneliness (UCLA Loneliness Scale). We will employ generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) to flexibly model non-linear dose-response curves and use piecewise regression to identify potential inflection points or thresholds in the density-outcome relationships.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of non-linear effects of PA density, moving beyond linear FITT assumptions. It will generate evidence to refine public health guidelines and clinical prescriptions by identifying optimal, efficient dosing patterns for cognitive and mental health benefits in aging.
proposals.novelty: While the concept of PA density has been proposed, its operationalization and the explicit test for non-linear or threshold effects on combined cognitive and psychosocial outcomes in older adults is novel. This integrates Kramer's recent work on density and bidirectional media-loneliness relationships into a unified experimental framework.

proposals.title: Personalized Temporal Signatures of Physical Activity: An Intensive Longitudinal and Micro-Randomized Trial Approach to Optimize Cognitive and Affective Outcomes in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Recent work has moved beyond simple FITT principles to consider the density and distribution of physical activity (PA) for brain health, but these models remain population-averaged. For older adults, the cognitive and affective benefits of PA may critically depend on intra-individual factors like daily stress, sleep quality, and social context (e.g., loneliness), making a one-size-fits-all timing prescription suboptimal. A personalized, context-aware model is needed to determine when PA is most efficacious for a given individual.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that person-specific, context-dependent models of PA timing, derived from intensive longitudinal data, will outperform population-averaged guidelines in predicting and improving acute cognitive performance and mood in older adults, and that these personalized timing recommendations can be effectively delivered via just-in-time adaptive interventions.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 older adults for a 60-day observational study using wrist-worn accelerometers, continuous glucose monitors (where applicable), and smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to capture PA, context (stress, loneliness, time-of-day), and outcomes (mood, cognitive tests). We will train personalized models using a two-stage approach: 1) a Random Forest classifier to identify important contextual features for positive outcomes for each individual, and 2) a Gaussian Process model to estimate the individualized dose-response relationship between PA timing and outcomes. Subsequently, a 30-day Micro-Randomized Trial (MRT) will test the efficacy of personalized timing suggestions delivered via smartphone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for moving from population-level PA 'density' guidelines to personalized, dynamic timing prescriptions. It will yield empirical evidence on the intra-individual moderators of PA efficacy and establish the feasibility of using MRTs to test adaptive PA interventions in real-world settings for older adults.
proposals.novelty: This integrates the concept of PA 'density' with idiographic, N-of-1 modeling and real-time intervention testing, moving beyond cross-sectional or aggregated longitudinal designs to model how the effects of PA timing vary within a person from day to day based on changing context.

proposals.title: Personalized Dynamic Biomarker Modeling for Just-in-Time Adaptive Physical Activity Prescription in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Current physical activity (PA) guidelines for cognitive health in older adults are based on population-level averages (e.g., FITT principles), ignoring individual variability in the neurobiological response dynamics to exercise. This one-size-fits-all approach fails to optimize the timing and dose of PA to sustain critical biomarker states, such as elevated prefrontal cortex oxygenation or neurotrophic factor levels, which are linked to cognitive benefits and reduced loneliness. A system that can identify and target these individual temporal dynamics is needed to move beyond generic prescriptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system identification approach, which builds personalized dynamic models of multi-modal biomarker responses to PA, will enable a Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) that more effectively achieves and sustains target neurobiological states associated with cognitive health in older adults, compared to standard fixed-dose prescriptions.
proposals.method: We will recruit older adults for a two-phase study. First, in a system identification phase, participants will complete structured PA bouts while we collect high-density wearable fNIRS (prefrontal oxygenation), mobile EEG (theta/beta rhythms), and serial salivary assays (BDNF, cortisol). We will use vector autoregressive (VAR) and nonlinear system identification techniques (e.g., NARMAX models) to characterize each individual's multi-biomarker response dynamics. Second, these models will inform a smartphone-delivered JITAI that uses real-time sensor data (heart rate, activity) to prescribe the timing, type, and intensity of PA micro-bouts to drive biomarker states toward personalized optimal zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for modeling the temporal dynamics of the brain's response to PA at an individual level. It will yield a novel, biologically-grounded JITAI system for personalized PA prescription. The findings will advance the field beyond static FITT-based guidelines by introducing a dynamic, closed-loop paradigm for cognitive health interventions.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct application of control-theoretic system identification to model multi-biomarker exercise responses for intervention design, moving decisively beyond the flawed 'drug' analogy for PA dosing. It integrates wearable neuroimaging into a real-time adaptive loop for cognitive health, a method not yet deployed in pre-2026 PA research.

proposals.title: From the Field to the Lab and Back: Translating Observed Daily Activity Cycles into Personalized Cognitive Health Guidelines for Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kramer's work, establishes that physical activity benefits cognitive health in older adults, but the FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) framework often fails to capture the complex density and recovery patterns of real-world activity. Current exercise guidelines are largely based on lab studies and athletic models, lacking ecological validity for the spontaneous, intermittent activity patterns of non-athletes, which may limit adherence and efficacy. This gap hinders the translation of sports science principles into feasible, population-informed public health guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) natural daily activity cycles in older adults can be empirically clustered into distinct 'interval' profiles, moderated by factors like baseline fitness and loneliness, and (2) a translation model mapping these real-world profiles to adapted sports science principles (e.g., density) will yield personalized activity guidance that is perceived as more feasible and leads to greater engagement and cognitive benefit than standard FITT-based recommendations.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+). Using a 14-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via smartphone prompts and wearable accelerometers/HR monitors, we will capture spontaneous activity bouts, rest periods, and perceived intensity. Unsupervised machine learning (Gaussian Mixture Models, Hidden Markov Models) will identify latent interval clusters. Moderated regression will test age, fitness (VO2 max estimate), loneliness (UCLA Loneliness Scale), and occupation as moderators. Finally, we will develop and validate a Bayesian translation model that inputs an individual's cluster and moderator data to output personalized, density-informed activity guidance, testing its feasibility and initial efficacy in a 4-week pilot RCT against a standard guideline control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven methodology for moving beyond one-size-fits-all FITT prescriptions to dynamic, personalized activity guidance grounded in observed behavior. It will contribute a validated translation model for adapting athletic interval principles to non-athlete populations safely. The findings will directly inform the next generation of cognitive health guidelines by incorporating real-world behavioral patterns and key psychosocial moderators like loneliness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined activity density or used wearables for measurement, this study uniquely inverts the translation pipeline: it first captures the population's natural 'interval' reality to then inform and adapt sports science principles, rather than imposing athletic models. It innovatively integrates behavioral clustering, psychosocial moderators, and a computational translation model specifically for cognitive health in aging.

proposals.title: Modeling the 24-Hour Behavioral Composition: A Compositional Data Analysis of the Trade-offs Between Sleep, Screen Time, and Physical Activity in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 public health guidelines for sleep, sedentary behavior, and physical activity are typically studied and prescribed in isolation, ignoring their inherent codependency within a finite 24-hour day. This siloed approach fails to capture the real-world behavioral trade-offs older adults make, limiting the ecological validity of interventions and our understanding of their combined impact on cognitive health and loneliness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative balance (composition) of sleep, screen-based sedentary time, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) within a 24-hour period, modeled as a coherent system, will be a stronger predictor of cognitive performance and perceived loneliness in older adults than the absolute duration of any single behavior.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+ years). Movement behaviors will be measured concurrently for 7 days using wrist-worn accelerometry (for sleep and PA) and smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (for screen time). The primary analysis will use Compositional Data Analysis (CoDA), treating the 24-hour cycle as a closed system. We will employ isometric log-ratio transformations to create compositional variables, which will then be used in linear regression models to predict outcomes from a pre-2026 cognitive battery and the UCLA Loneliness Scale, controlling for covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, integrative framework for understanding 24-hour movement behaviors in aging, moving beyond independent FITT principles. It will yield practical, composition-based guidelines that acknowledge behavioral trade-offs, directly informing more holistic and feasible public health recommendations and interventions for cognitive and social well-being in older adults.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kramer and others has examined bidirectional relationships between specific behaviors (e.g., TV and loneliness), this study innovates by applying CoDA—a method emerging in physical activity epidemiology pre-2026—to model sleep, screen time, and PA as a single, interactive system influencing cognitive and psychosocial outcomes.

proposals.title: Dose-Response Relationships Between 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Social-Emotional Function in Older Adults: A Multi-Method Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Kramer and others highlights the complex interplay between physical activity, sedentary behavior (e.g., screen time), and cognitive/social health in aging. However, studies often examine these behaviors in isolation, neglecting their integrated, dose-response effects on critical social-emotional outcomes like loneliness and emotional regulation. Understanding the precise, non-linear relationships within the 24-hour activity cycle is essential for developing nuanced public health guidelines for older adults.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, non-linear dose-response curves exist for physical activity, sleep quality/duration, and recreational screen time in relation to social-emotional function (loneliness, perceived social support, affect), and that optimal combinations of these behaviors will predict significantly better outcomes than any single behavior.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+). Movement behaviors will be objectively measured for 7 days using tri-axial accelerometers (ActiGraph) and sleep diaries. Social-emotional function will be assessed via validated questionnaires (e.g., UCLA Loneliness Scale, PANAS) and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via smartphone prompts. Dose-response relationships will be analyzed using multivariate adaptive regression splines (MARS) and compositional data analysis (CoDA) to model non-linear effects and the interdependence of activity, sleep, and sedentary time within a 24-hour framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, dose-response maps linking the full spectrum of daily movement behaviors to social-emotional health in older adults. It will move the field beyond simple FITT principles by applying advanced compositional and non-linear modeling, directly informing more precise public health activity guidelines that consider behavioral trade-offs and synergies.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively integrates Kramer's work on physical activity dose-response and screen time/social health by applying compositional data analysis to model the 24-hour activity cycle, testing for specific inflection points in behavior-outcome relationships rather than assuming linearity.

proposals.title: Objective 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Loneliness in Older Adults: A Triangulated, Device-Based Investigation
proposals.motivation: Prior work, including Kramer's team's study on TV viewing and loneliness, relies heavily on self-report, which is prone to recall and social desirability biases. Understanding the precise, objective composition of daily movement behaviors (physical activity, sedentary time/screen use, sleep) is critical for developing effective, evidence-based public health guidelines to combat loneliness, a major risk factor for cognitive and physical decline in aging populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objectively measured patterns of 24-hour movement—specifically, higher moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), lower device-logged screen time, and higher sleep efficiency—will be independently and interactively associated with lower levels of loneliness in older adults, even after controlling for relevant psychosocial covariates.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling adults aged 60-80. Participants will wear a tri-axial accelerometer (ActiGraph GT9X) on the wrist for 14 days to objectively capture physical activity intensity and sleep parameters (using the Cole-Kripke algorithm). Screen time will be logged via smartphone usage statistics (iOS Screen Time, Android Digital Wellbeing). Loneliness will be assessed using the validated UCLA Loneliness Scale (Version 3). Data will be analyzed using compositional data analysis (CoDA) and isotemporal substitution models to understand the interdependent relationships within the 24-hour movement cycle.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first device-based, compositional analysis of the 24-hour movement cycle in relation to loneliness in older adults. It will move the field beyond simple FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) principles by offering precise, objective evidence to inform future public health guidelines that target specific, modifiable daily behaviors to reduce loneliness and promote cognitive health.
proposals.novelty: While Kramer's past work established a TV-loneliness link via self-report, this proposal innovates by using a multi-device, objective assessment of the full 24-hour cycle and applying CoDA, a statistical framework that respects the interdependent nature of time-use data, to model complex behavioral trade-offs.

proposals.title: Predicting Adherence to 24-Hour Movement Guidelines from Social-Emotional Profiles in Older Adults: An Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: While 24-hour movement guidelines (integrating physical activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep) are established for older adults' health, adherence is poor and its predictors are not fully understood. Pre-2026 work by Kramer and others links physical activity with cognitive and emotional function, but the inverse relationship—using an individual's current social-emotional function to predict their likelihood of adhering to movement guidelines—remains unexplored. This 'inverse problem' approach could identify key psychosocial barriers to guideline adherence, enabling more personalized and effective behavioral interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific profiles of social-emotional function (e.g., loneliness, trait mindfulness, self-control) will significantly predict adherence to integrated 24-hour movement guidelines in older adults, beyond traditional demographic and health covariates.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+ years) and assess their social-emotional function using validated scales for loneliness, mindfulness, self-control, and depressive symptoms. Adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines (e.g., WHO/Canadian guidelines) will be objectively measured for one week using tri-axial accelerometers and sleep diaries. We will employ a machine learning pipeline, using Random Forest and Gradient Boosting classifiers (scikit-learn v0.24), to model adherence (binary outcome: meets/does not meet guidelines) from the psychosocial profile, controlling for age, sex, and BMI. Model performance will be evaluated via cross-validated AUC-ROC, and SHAP values will identify the most predictive functional features.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, person-centered model for identifying older adults at high risk of non-adherence to holistic movement guidelines based on their psychosocial state. It will extend the FITT/density framework by integrating psychosocial predictors into the dose-response model of activity and health. The findings will inform the development of targeted interventions that address specific social-emotional barriers to improve guideline adherence and, consequently, cognitive and brain health.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical causal direction examined in the field, modeling movement behavior as an outcome of psychosocial state rather than a cause. It uniquely applies a machine learning 'inverse problem' framework to the integrated 24-hour movement guidelines, moving beyond studying physical activity in isolation.

proposals.title: Beyond Attenuation: Quantifying the Positive Dose-Response Relationship Between Physical Activity and Cognitive Resilience in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on aging and cognition predominantly focuses on how physical activity mitigates cognitive decline and deficits. This deficit-oriented model overlooks a critical question: can regular movement behaviors actively build cognitive strengths and resilience, even in the absence of significant pathology? Understanding physical activity as a builder of positive cognitive capital, rather than just a reducer of losses, is essential for developing strength-based interventions and public health guidelines for healthy aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, objectively measured profiles of physical activity (considering intensity, frequency, and temporal density) will show specific, positive dose-response relationships with metrics of cognitive resilience, including cognitive reserve, processing speed gains, and emotional regulation under stress, independent of their association with reduced cognitive difficulties.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month longitudinal study with 300 cognitively healthy older adults (65-80 yrs). Physical activity will be measured continuously using tri-axial accelerometers (ActiGraph GT9X) to derive metrics of intensity, frequency, and bout density (e.g., using the sojourn method). Cognitive resilience will be operationalized via a latent variable from a battery including the Cognitive Reserve Index questionnaire, repeated cognitive testing (NIH Toolbox) to measure intra-individual variability and learning, and stress-reactive tasks assessing executive function. Data will be analyzed using latent growth curve modeling and machine learning techniques (specifically, random forest regression with SHAP values) to identify which PA profile features most strongly predict resilience outcomes, controlling for baseline cognition, loneliness, and other covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for conceptualizing physical activity as a builder of cognitive strengths in aging. It will move the field beyond the FITT principle by demonstrating how the temporal structure of activity relates to positive cognitive outcomes. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation physical activity guidelines aimed at promoting cognitive vitality and resilience, not just preventing decline.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant deficit model in cognitive aging research by explicitly testing for positive, strength-building outcomes of physical activity. It integrates advanced activity density metrics with a multi-faceted operationalization of cognitive resilience, applying machine learning to move beyond linear correlations and identify complex, predictive profiles of protective movement behaviors.

proposals.title: Network Dynamics of Daily Behaviors: A Psychopathology-Informed Analysis of Sleep, Physical Activity, and Screen Time on Cognitive and Psychological Outcomes in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kramer's work, establishes that physical activity, sedentary screen time, and sleep are individually linked to cognitive health and loneliness in aging. However, these behaviors co-occur and likely influence each other in daily life, creating a complex system that traditional linear models (e.g., FITT) cannot fully capture. Understanding the interconnected network of these behaviors is crucial for developing holistic, effective lifestyle guidelines for older adults.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sleep quality, moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), and recreational screen time form a stable, interconnected network in older adults, where changes in one node (e.g., increased screen time) will predictably impact others (e.g., reduced sleep quality), and that the overall structure and strength of this network will be directly associated with levels of perceived loneliness and performance on cognitive control tasks.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 community-dwelling older adults (65+). For 14 days, participants will wear accelerometers to objectively measure sleep and MVPA and complete daily diaries for screen time and loneliness. Pre- and post-assessment will include cognitive tests (executive function, processing speed). Using R and the `qgraph` package, we will construct Gaussian Graphical Models (GGMs) to estimate partial correlation networks among the daily behavioral and outcome variables. We will compute network metrics (e.g., centrality, edge weights) and use the Network Comparison Test to examine differences between high- and low-loneliness subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond examining behaviors in isolation by providing a systems-level map of how key lifestyle factors interact in real time. It will identify the most central, potentially influential behaviors in the network, offering novel targets for multi-behavior intervention. The application of psychopathological network methods to normative aging and lifestyle research represents a significant methodological advance for the field.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in psychopathology, its application to model the dynamic interplay of everyday health behaviors in aging is novel. This approach directly addresses the call in Dr. Kramer's recent work to move 'beyond FITT' by modeling density and interconnectedness, rather than isolated dose-response relationships.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimization of 24-Hour Movement Profiles to Predict Academic Engagement and Resilience in Emerging Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on physical activity and cognition, led by researchers like Kramer, has established dose-response relationships but typically examines single behaviors in isolation. The 24-hour movement paradigm (integrating sleep, sedentary behavior, and physical activity) is gaining traction, yet the field lacks methods to identify the specific, holistic behavioral profiles that are most predictive of key real-world outcomes like academic engagement and psychological resilience. This inverse approach is critical for translating research into personalized behavioral guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: Distinct, optimal 24-hour movement profiles—characterized by specific temporal patterns and intensities of sleep, sedentary time, light activity, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity—can be identified that are uniquely predictive of high levels of academic engagement and resilience, and these profiles will differ meaningfully from population-averaged guidelines.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 emerging adults (18-25 years) and use a multi-modal assessment: 7-day accelerometry (ActiGraph) combined with ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via smartphone to capture 24-hour movement and real-time academic engagement. Resilience will be measured via validated scales (e.g., Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale). The core analysis will employ a two-stage machine learning approach: 1) Use k-means clustering on accelerometry-derived features (e.g., timing, duration, intensity of behaviors) to identify latent movement profiles. 2) Apply random forest classification and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) value analysis to solve the 'inverse problem'—determining which specific profile features are most predictive of high engagement and resilience outcomes, moving beyond linear regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for moving from generalized public health guidelines (e.g., FITT) to personalized behavioral prescriptions. It will yield specific, empirically-derived 24-hour movement profiles associated with positive cognitive and psychological outcomes in a young adult population, directly informing the next generation of targeted interventions.
